_doc: Greenroom Dispatch — weekly stand-up news. weeks[0] is current (home section reads it: items[0]=hero, items[1..]=briefs). Every item: real, dated, sourced, never fabricated. Fields: kind,date,dateShort,headline,dek (one short sentence, shown on brief cards),synopsis (a fuller 3-4 sentence paragraph; shown as the HERO preview card body to fill its tall column, and in every popup),source,url,comicId,comic,photo. Give the HERO (items[0]) a rich synopsis so its preview card fills. Generated/updated by the 'greenroom' scheduled task (draft-gated).

items.headline: Patton Oswalt drops a new hour, “Tea & Scotch,” free on YouTube
items.dek: His latest special, recorded in Madison, premieres free on 800 Pound Gorilla’s YouTube channel June 9.
items.synopsis: Patton Oswalt premieres his latest stand-up hour, “Tea & Scotch,” at 8 p.m. ET on June 9, streaming free on 800 Pound Gorilla Media’s YouTube channel. Recorded in Madison, Wisconsin and presented by Super Nice Guys, the set runs through parenting, artificial intelligence, his thirty years on Prozac, and a stretch about showing his teenage daughter John Carpenter’s “Halloween” for the first time. It is a deliberately low-friction release for one of his generation’s most prolific comics, betting a marquee name can still draw a crowd on a free platform. Oswalt remains a constant on the live circuit between film and voice work.

items.headline: Hannah Berner’s second special “None of My Business” lands on Hulu
items.dek: Her sophomore hour, filmed in Toronto, premiered June 5 on Hulu.
items.synopsis: Hannah Berner released her second stand-up special, “None of My Business,” on Hulu on June 5, the streamer’s first hour from the “Giggly Squad” co-host and former “Summer House” cast member. Filmed at Toronto’s Bluma Appel Theatre and directed by Jeff Tomsic, the set digs into her unorthodox path from pro tennis and reality TV to comedy, along with dating and her uncertainty about the future. It follows her 70-show “None of My Business” tour and her 2024 Netflix debut “We Ride at Dawn.” Berner told Variety that stand-up felt “healing” after reality television.

items.headline: Gabriel Iglesias and Jo Koy co-headline London’s O2 Arena
items.dek: The two arena draws shared a one-night-only O2 bill June 6, their first London team-up.
items.synopsis: Gabriel “Fluffy” Iglesias and Jo Koy brought a one-night-only show to London’s O2 Arena on June 6, the first time the two co-headlined together in the U.K. capital. The pairing follows their sold-out turn at Los Angeles’ SoFi Stadium, where they became the first comedians to fill the venue. Both built global followings on family-rooted storytelling, Iglesias through character voices and sound effects, Koy through his Filipino-American upbringing. The O2 date underscores how far the touring stand-up business has scaled into arenas and stadiums.

items.headline: KevOnStage signs Tubi’s biggest creator deal yet
items.dek: The YouTube comic’s multi-year, multi-project Tubi slate includes his first major-streamer stand-up special.
items.synopsis: Tubi announced its largest creator partnership to date on June 4, a multi-year, multi-project slate with comedian and digital creator Kevin “KevOnStage” Fredericks. The deal covers two more seasons of his series “Safe Space,” a new workplace comedy called “The Airport,” two feature films, and his first stand-up special for a major streamer, “Grief Sucks,” set for February 2027. Fredericks keeps ownership and creative control, a structure he called “unheard of.” It marks how far native YouTube and digital comics have pushed into traditional streaming.

items.headline: Scott Aukerman’s “Comedy Bang! Bang!” re-ups with SiriusXM
items.dek: One of comedy’s longest-running podcasts inked a multi-year SiriusXM extension June 1.
items.synopsis: Comedy Bang! Bang!, the improvised interview podcast hosted by writer-comedian Scott Aukerman, signed a multi-year extension with SiriusXM, Deadline reported June 1. The deal keeps one of the world’s longest-running comedy podcasts, a launchpad for absurdist character work since 2009, under the SiriusXM umbrella for production, distribution, and ad sales. The show seeded a generation of alt-comedy performers and spun off an IFC television series. The renewal signals continued audio-platform appetite for established comedy franchises.

items.headline: “Workaholics” alums renew “This Is Important” with iHeart
items.dek: Adam Devine, Anders Holm and Blake Anderson re-signed their comedy podcast in a multi-year iHeart deal.
items.synopsis: iHeartPodcasts and Mail Order Company, the studio of “Workaholics” alums Adam Devine, Anders Holm and Blake Anderson, set a multi-year renewal for their comedy podcast “This Is Important,” Deadline reported June 4. The weekly audio-and-video show reunites the trio for loose, riff-heavy conversation, and iHeart said it would expand its production and marketing investment. The renewal extends one of the more durable comedian-led podcasts built on an existing fan base. It is another sign of the format consolidating around proven hosts.

items.headline: Nate Bargatze makes his big-screen debut in “The Breadwinner”
items.dek: The arena-filling stand-up’s first studio lead opened in theaters May 29.
items.synopsis: Nate Bargatze, the clean-comedy juggernaut behind the “Big Dumb Eyes” world tour, took his deadpan to the multiplex on May 29 with “The Breadwinner,” his first studio film as a leading man. Directed by Eric Appel and co-written by Bargatze with Dan Lagana, the Sony comedy casts him as a father of three left to run the household while his wife travels for work. Critics were unconvinced: Variety and The Hollywood Reporter both filed tepid reviews, and the film opened to a middling score on the aggregators. Still, it is a milestone, the moment one of the biggest names in live stand-up tests whether that audience follows him to the screen.

items.headline: Mo Amer hosts the 86th Peabody Awards
items.dek: The “Mo” creator and stand-up emcees the Peabodys on May 31, a medallion he once won.
items.synopsis: Mo Amer, the Houston stand-up and creator-star of Netflix’s Peabody-winning “Mo,” took over hosting duties for the 86th Peabody Awards on May 31 at the Beverly Wilshire in Beverly Hills. It is a full-circle turn for a comic who collected the same award for his own series and was nominated again this year. The 2026 class of honorees includes Amy Poehler for career achievement and James L. Brooks for the inaugural Industry Icon award.

items.headline: Russell Peters brings the “Relax World Tour” to the U.S.
items.dek: The crowd-work veteran’s all-new world tour hit the Northeast in late May.
items.synopsis: Russell Peters rolled his “Relax World Tour,” billed as his biggest and most expansive run yet, into the U.S. Northeast in late May, with a May 29 stop at NJPAC in Newark and a May 30 date on Long Island. The set leans on new material and the razor crowd work that built him into an arena draw worldwide, following legs from Mumbai to London. It is the latest chapter for one of stand-up’s original globe-spanning headliners.

items.headline: Ben Gleib launches “Good Night,” a YouTube-native late-night show
items.dek: The stand-up’s talk show, built for YouTube and taped at home, premiered May 28.
items.synopsis: Comedian Ben Gleib debuted “Good Night with Ben Gleib” on May 28, pitching it as the first late-night talk show conceived natively for YouTube rather than ported from television. Gleib tapes the weekly show in a converted room of his San Fernando Valley house, with a 42-episode first season and former “Daily Show” producer Stewart Bailey as showrunner. Opening guests included filmmaker Kevin Smith and comics Zainab Johnson and Brent Pella. The launch is a bet that the late-night format can survive off-network, on a creator’s own channel.

items.headline: Jen Kober sets her debut special “Uber Famous”
items.dek: The Louisiana comic’s first full-length hour arrives June 2 via Comedy Dynamics.
items.synopsis: Jen Kober will release her first full-length stand-up special, “Uber Famous,” through Comedy Dynamics on June 2. Filmed in her hometown of Lake Charles, Louisiana, and directed by Jeff DeRouen, the hour turns stories of family, identity, and unexpected fame into what she calls her most honest and joyful set. A rotating headliner on Cirque du Soleil’s Las Vegas show “Mad Apple” and an NPR “Snap Judgment” favorite, Kober has spent two decades as a road and improv force; this is her overdue first headline hour.

items.headline: Matt Mathews announces his first world tour
items.dek: The viral comic’s “Not What I Ordered” tour sets 74 dates across four countries.
items.synopsis: Matt Mathews, the Alabama comedian who broke out with viral “Farm Chores” videos and now counts more than 15 million followers, announced his first world tour, “Not What I Ordered.” The 74-date run stretches from September 2026 into May 2027 and marks his first international outing, with stops across the U.S., U.K., Australia, and New Zealand. Variety broke the news as the tour opened its presale.

items.headline: Wanda Sykes returns to Netflix with ‘Legacy’
items.dek: Her third Netflix hour, filmed at her alma mater Hampton University, premiered May 19.
items.synopsis: Wanda Sykes’s third Netflix hour, “Legacy,” premiered May 19 — and the setting is part of the story. She taped it at Hampton University, the historically Black college in Virginia she attended before stand-up, turning a campus homecoming into the show’s frame. Directed by Julie Dash (“Daughters of the Dust”) and produced through Push It Productions, the company Sykes runs with Page Hurwitz, it lands two years after “I’m an Entertainer.”

items.headline: Josh Johnson’s first HBO special, ‘Symphony,’ arrives
items.dek: The Daily Show writer-correspondent’s debut HBO hour premiered May 22, filmed at the Wiltern.
items.synopsis: “Symphony,” Josh Johnson’s first HBO special, premiered May 22, filmed at the Wiltern in Los Angeles. A rotating Daily Show host, Johnson built an enormous online following in 2025 by releasing topical sets at a relentless pace; this is his first network hour.

items.headline: Mike Epps signs a two-special Netflix deal
items.dek: Following “Delusional,” Epps inked a deal for two more Netflix stand-up specials.
items.synopsis: Mike Epps has signed with Netflix for two additional stand-up specials, the platform announced in mid-May. The expansion follows his fifth Netflix hour, “Delusional,” which drew his strongest opening week yet.

items.headline: Anjelah Johnson-Reyes drops ‘Ugly Baby’ free on YouTube
items.dek: Her self-produced seventh special premiered free on YouTube on Mother’s Day, May 10.
items.synopsis: Anjelah Johnson-Reyes released her seventh stand-up special, “Ugly Baby,” free on YouTube on Mother’s Day, May 10. Her self-financed directorial debut, filmed at LA’s El Rey Theatre with 800 Pound Gorilla, centers on her path to motherhood — fertility, pregnancy, and postpartum life.

items.headline: Sinbad returns to stand-up after his 2020 stroke
items.dek: The comedian played his first set in over five years, with a second date on May 10.
items.synopsis: Sinbad returned to the stand-up stage April 29 at the Comedy Ice House in Pasadena — his first performance since a debilitating 2020 stroke — with a second sold-out date on May 10. His daughter, comedian Paige Bryan, opened, alongside Chase Anthony.

items.headline: Netflix Is A Joke Fest takes over Los Angeles
items.dek: The festival ran May 4–10 across dozens of LA venues with hundreds of shows.
items.synopsis: Netflix Is A Joke Fest returned for its third edition, May 4–10, staging hundreds of shows across dozens of Los Angeles venues from the Hollywood Bowl to the Comedy Store. Headliners ranged from Dave Chappelle and John Mulaney to Nikki Glaser, Shane Gillis, and Katt Williams.

items.headline: Bill Maher named the 2026 Mark Twain Prize recipient
items.dek: The Kennedy Center will present its top humor honor to Maher on June 28.
items.synopsis: Bill Maher is the 2026 recipient of the Mark Twain Prize for American Humor, the Kennedy Center confirmed, with the ceremony set for June 28 and a later Netflix stream. Past honorees include Conan O’Brien, Dave Chappelle, Eddie Murphy, and Carol Burnett.
